text: The video displays the port placement for a hand-assisted nephrectomy on the right side, showing the arrangement of surgical ports on the patient's abdomen.
The patient is positioned in a beanbag in a full-flank position. The surgeon makes a small seven-centimeter muscle-splitting incision to place the hand port for the nephrectomy procedure.
Two additional 10mm ports are placed, including one inside the umbilicus. The surgeon uses a 3mm alligator grasper to elevate the liver, providing better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the patient's abdomen, revealing the visible arrangement of abdominal organs.
The surgeon inserts their hand through the hand port, which is positioned on the right side for hand-assisted nephrectomy.
The surgeon ensures the hand port is sealed around their hand to maintain a proper laparoscopic insufflation seal during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions a three-millimeter alligator grasper as a self-retaining retractor to elevate the liver, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon visually inspects the abdominal cavity, where the gallbladder, liver, and kidney are clearly visible, especially in this thin patient.
The surgeon begins the nephrectomy by performing a formal takedown of the line of Toldt, which involves mobilizing the colon to access the kidney.
The surgeon uses their hand as a retractor to stabilize and expose the kidney, enhancing visibility and access during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon rapidly dissects along the line of Toldt, visibly separating the colon from the retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon extends the dissection towards the side of the liver and up to the diaphragm, preparing the area for nephrectomy.
The surgeon positions their hand over the top of the kidney to sweep the surrounding Gerota's fascia and fat downwards, facilitating exposure of the kidney during the nephrectomy.
The kidney becomes fully visible in the surgical field, simplifying the procedure as the surgeon continues the nephrectomy.
The surgeon uses their hand to sweep surrounding tissues downwards, demonstrating the advantage of manual manipulation in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum along the edge of the liver, extending the incision towards the diaphragm.
The surgeon positions their hand over the top of the kidney, preparing to manipulate surrounding tissues.
The surgeon proceeds to take down the duodenum and colon, following the previous steps that exposed the kidney and allowed for downward sweeping of the Girotis fat.
The surgeon uses their hand as a retractor to maintain downward pressure, aiding in the exposure of the kidney.
The surgeon demonstrates the technique for dissecting the kidney during a partial nephrectomy, focusing on precise tissue separation around the kidney.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical plane where the colon rests upon the Gerota's fat, carefully delineating the tissue layers in preparation for further dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon downwards, creating space and exposing the underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon exposes the bowel, likely the duodenum, situated beneath the colon during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses fingers as a dissector to manipulate and hold the tissue, functioning as a retractor during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses their hand to dissect and create retraction points, manipulating the surrounding tissue to facilitate exposure.
The surgeon uses two fingers to retract tissue, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses their pinky finger to push the kidney laterally while simultaneously using their thumb to push the bowel medially, manipulating these structures to improve surgical exposure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to access the surgical plane, ensuring the duodenum is moved out of the way, visible beneath the colon.
The surgeon systematically identifies anatomical planes and structures, using hand-assisted techniques to reveal them progressively within the surgical field.
The renal vein becomes visible as the surgeon continues to manipulate surrounding tissues, revealing its location in the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the lower pole of the kidney and completes the mobilization of the colon, with the duodenum and bowel positioned downward.
The surgeon continues the dissection, extending the surgical field down to the pelvis, ensuring clear visualization of anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses their hand to hold the kidney in an upright position, providing a clear view of the organ during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the vena cava, visible in the surgical field as part of the nephrectomy procedure.
An aberrant vessel is visible near the renal vein, distinct from the expected anatomical structures.
A crossing vessel is visible, identified as a non-pathologic structure, crossing over the surgical field.
The surgeon holds the kidney upright with one hand, but refrains from dissecting it, maintaining the current position without further manipulation.
The surgeon holds up the lower pole of the kidney while completing the dissection around the colon, ensuring clear exposure of the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon advances along the vena cava, ensuring not to move more medially than necessary.
The surgeon identifies a small crossing vessel near the kidney, which may be an aberrant gonadal vessel or another vessel associated with the kidney.
The surgeon positions onto the psoas muscle and begins to move upwards along its surface, revealing surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon approaches the renal vein, previously identified, within the surgical field, preparing to demonstrate its anatomical position and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies several aberrant vessels in the surgical field, which are visually distinct and do not typically belong in the expected anatomical location.
The surgeon identifies a small crossing artery and vein, possibly aberrant vessels, near the kidney.
The surgeon holds the kidney straight up using the thumb and forefinger, revealing a clear plane of fascia with the duodenum positioned downward.
The surgeon anticipates visualizing the plane beneath which the main renal vein is clearly visible, preparing to expose this anatomical structure during the nephrectomy.
The surgeon follows a predictable fascial plane located one level beneath the plane developed for duodenum mobilization, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon approaches the top of the kidney, preparing to dissect across this area, with the kidney held in position for clear visibility.
The surgeon releases the gyrotis fat near the kidney, aiming to spare the adrenal gland during the nephrectomy procedure.
The renal vein is visible branching from the main vessel, indicating the anatomical location and vascular structure during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon gently manipulates the tissue to expose the parenchyma of the kidney, ensuring that no vascular structures remain in the field of view.
The surgeon creates windows in the Girotis fat at the upper pole of the kidney, having previously swept the fat down to expose the area.
The surgeon prepares to staple the Girotis fat, noting that the adrenal gland is located within this fat near the vena cava.
The surgeon observes the fat tissue, noting that it can often bleed during manipulation.
The surgeon uses both hands to carefully dissect around the kidney, ensuring precise manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the fat at the upper pole of the kidney.
The surgeon confirms the preservation of the patient's adrenal gland during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon has reached the upper pole of the kidney and is now holding the kidney straight up, preparing for the final steps of the nephrectomy.
The surgeon places fingers behind the renal hilum and begins dissecting out the visible vessels surrounding the renal vein.
The surgeon works on a crossing vessel located inferiorly, carefully dissecting around it to ensure clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon creates retraction points around the kidney, facilitating better exposure and manipulation during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses their thumb as a retractor to hold the kidney straight up, facilitating further dissection during the nephrectomy.
The surgeon positions their middle finger around the renal hilum and applies pressure to the hilum, aiming to manipulate the kidney during the nephrectomy.
The surgeon manipulates the hilum, causing the renal artery to emerge from beneath the renal vein, revealing the spatial relationship between these vessels.
The surgeon dissects around the vessels, carefully isolating each one using precise movements.
The surgeon applies pressure on the artery, visibly demonstrating its position and separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses their finger to create a window around the hilum, preparing to suck over the finger for further dissection.
The surgeon proceeds retroperitoneally along the psoas muscle, navigating the anatomical space adjacent to the kidney.
The surgeon mobilizes the kidney, manipulating it to achieve a position where it can be held straight upwards, indicating complete detachment from surrounding structures.
The psoas muscle is visible in the surgical field, providing a landmark for further dissection during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the kidney to visualize all surrounding vessels, ensuring clear identification of each vessel in the surgical field.
The surgeon releases the lateral attachments of the kidney, preparing to perform additional work on the hilum.
The surgeon visually identifies the ureter for the first time during the procedure, highlighting its location in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an aberrant gonadal vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon manipulates a structure that is not a vessel, distinguishing it from surrounding vascular elements.
The surgeon identifies an aberrant gonadal vessel along with a crossing artery and vein in the surgical field.
The surgeon's finger is visibly passed completely underneath an anatomical structure, likely an aberrant vessel, as part of the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to isolate and secure the artery on the right side, which is typically the final step in this part of the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses their finger to carefully pull a structure out from underneath the vein, demonstrating the separation of tissues.
The surgeon continues to manipulate the tissue, gradually bringing the artery into clearer view as it is separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon positions their finger underneath the vein, creating a visible space or 'window' for the insertion of a surgical stapler.

The surgeon positions their finger on both sides of the kidney, creating a clear window for the stapler. The kidney is held up to ensure no obstructions are present behind it, facilitating the stapling process.
The surgeon successfully creates a window around the kidney, visible enough to insert a finger through the space, ensuring clear access for the stapler.
The surgeon demonstrates a clear window beneath the kidney, indicating readiness to pass the stapler through this space without obstruction.
The surgeon maintains upward traction on the kidney, ensuring a clear view and access for the stapler placement.
The surgeon maintains tension on the vessels, ensuring they are clearly visible and accessible for further manipulation or stapling.
The surgeon visually confirms that the identified structures are not connected to the mesentery of the bowel.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to address the lymphatic vessels, applying the instrument to cut and coagulate the tissue.
The surgeon prepares the kidney for staple firing, ensuring the surrounding tissue is adequately positioned for the next step in the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the arteries, visually distinguishing them from surrounding structures in preparation for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon identifies the veins associated with the kidney, preparing for further dissection or ligation.
The surgeon identifies several aberrant arteries and veins, which are gradually exposed and managed through the steps of a hand-assisted nephrectomy procedure.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon begins to dissect tissue adjacent to the ureter, leaving only the necessary structures for removal.
The surgeon uses small metal clips to secure and occlude several small aberrant vessels surrounding the kidney, preparing for further steps in the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to ligate the identified arteries, prioritizing their removal before addressing the veins.
The main renal artery is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an aberrant crossing artery, preparing to ligate it as the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies an atypical artery, which is not commonly observed in most nephrectomy cases.
The surgeon observes the remaining veins in the surgical field after previous dissection steps.
The surgeon carefully dissects around a vessel near the inferior vena cava, ensuring to address the vessel before it branches.
The surgical field reveals a few remaining lymphatic vessels and one visible vein, which are left after previous dissection steps.
The surgeon isolates the vessels, focusing on separating them from surrounding tissues during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon ligates and removes the vein, leaving the ureter as the remaining structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, which is the last structure to be dissected and removed in the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon presents the excised kidney specimen, displaying its anatomical features and size after removal.
The surgeon pulls the kidney specimen straight out of the abdomen, completing the extraction process.
The surgeon places a sponge in the surgical field to address minimal bleeding observed during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon inspects the surgical stumps, examining the area where the kidney was removed for any signs of bleeding or other abnormalities.
The surgeon reduces the insufflation pressure to five, visually confirming the absence of oozing or bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the staple line on the renal vein, indicating the completion of vessel ligation during the nephrectomy procedure.
The vena cava is visible in the surgical field, likely adjacent to the renal vein staple line.
The surgeon visually identifies the accessory artery and the stump of the main renal artery, inspecting these structures for any signs of bleeding or complications.
The surgeon inspects the stumps of the accessory artery and renal vein, confirming visually that there is no leakage from these structures.
The surgeon displays the bivalve kidney, showing the internal structure and anatomy of the kidney in this nephrectomy case.
The video shows a centrally located tumor within the kidney, highlighting its position relative to surrounding renal structures.
